title: Generic K3 categories and Hodge theory
speaker: Daniel Huybrechts
abstract: In this talk I will focus on two examples of K3 categories: bounded derived categories of (twisted) coherent sheaves and K3 categories associated with smooth cubic fourfolds. The group of autoequivalences of the former has been intensively studied over the years (work by Mukai, Orlov, Bridgeland and others), whereas the investigation of the latter has only just began. As a motivation, I shall recall Mukai's classification of finite groups of automorphisms of K3 surfaces and its more recent derived version which involves the Leech lattice.

proof_stages.label: Kuznetsov component and period-lattice setup
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a smooth cubic, the speaker isolates the Kuznetsov K3 category A_X, poses the problem of understanding its exact k-linear autoequivalences for general X, and embeds the cubic period domain into the extended K3 lattice via the A2-orthogonal complement.

proof_stages.label: K3 and twisted K3 points in the extended period domain
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines K3 and twisted K3 points in the generalized K3 period domain and characterizes them by the existence of a primitive hyperbolic plane U, respectively a twisted hyperbolic plane U(n), in the integral (1,1)-part.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic loci of cubic periods of K3 type
proof_stages.summary: Intersecting these K3 and twisted K3 loci with the cubic period domain gives Hassett Noether–Lefschetz divisors, with explicit discriminant conditions, and the Addington–Thomas Mukai/Hodge structure on A_X identifies these loci with cubics whose K3 category has the Hodge structure of a K3 or twisted K3 surface.

proof_stages.label: Twisted K3 Mukai Hodge structures and derived Torelli
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the B-field construction of the twisted Mukai Hodge structure, the category of alpha-twisted coherent sheaves, and the Mukai–Orlov/Huybrechts–Stellari derived Torelli theorem identifying derived equivalences with orientation-preserving Hodge isometries.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological image of autoequivalence groups
proof_stages.summary: Autoequivalences of K3 or twisted K3 categories act on the Mukai Hodge lattice, and known Torelli-type results describe the image as orientation-preserving Hodge isometries, with finite symplectic subgroups related to Mukai’s M23 theorem and its derived Conway-group analogue.

proof_stages.label: Kernel control by roots
proof_stages.summary: The kernel of the cohomological representation is recalled: in the absence of (-2)-roots in the (1,1)-Mukai lattice it is generated by the double shift, while in untwisted Picard-rank-one cases Bayer–Bridgeland show a more complicated root-controlled kernel.

proof_stages.label: Bridgeland-type conjecture for cubic K3 categories
proof_stages.summary: For the cubic category A_X the speaker formulates an expected exact sequence whose kernel is the fundamental group of the complement of root hyperplanes in the complexified (1,1)-lattice and whose image is the orientation-preserving Hodge-isometry group, while explaining the unresolved issues of Fourier–Mukai representability and stability conditions.

proof_stages.label: Generic-cubic results via deformation to twisted K3 loci
proof_stages.summary: The announced in-progress strategy proves the full Hodge-theoretic image for generic cubics and a very small kernel for very general cubics by deforming Fourier–Mukai kernels to open sets meeting twisted K3 loci with no roots, applying the no-root kernel theorem there, and specializing back to the original cubic.

examples.name: Divisibility by 2 in the discriminant condition
examples.note: The speaker uses the case that 2 may divide the discriminant d but 4 may not, while 4 may divide 2d, to clarify the prime-power condition for twisted K3 cubic periods.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly checks how the twisted discriminant condition treats the prime 2, noting that 2 may divide d but not 4, while using 2d changes the divisibility by 4.

examples.name: Line bundles as minus-two Mukai classes
examples.note: Line bundles on an untwisted K3 surface are mentioned to make concrete why the root set in the one-one Mukai lattice is never empty in the untwisted case.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only states that every line bundle gives a minus-two class in the one-one Mukai part, without deriving or using a specific line bundle.

passes.reason: a line bundle gives a spherical Mukai vector of square minus two and is cited to show the no-root hypothesis fails for untwisted K3 surfaces

examples.name: Rigid vector bundles surviving a twist
examples.note: In response to a question, rigid vector bundles are cited as possible sources of root classes that can remain after twisting, illustrating why twisted K3 categories can still have roots on a dense set.
examples.engagement_reason: rigid vector bundles are only invoked as possible minus-two classes that might survive twisting, with no construction or check performed.

passes.reason: rigid vector bundles have minimal deformation space and are invoked to show that twisting need not eliminate all minus-two roots
rigid K3 bundles are spherical objects with square-minus-two Mukai vectors/minimal zero-dimensional moduli, invoked to show twisting need not remove all roots
Rigid bundles have zero-dimensional deformation behavior and can survive twisting as roots, obstructing the hope that twisting kills all minus-two classes.
Rigid bundles correspond to minimal/spherical minus-two classes and are cited as roots that may persist after twisting.
rigid vector bundles are minimal-deformation spherical objects whose survival under twisting obstructs the claim that twisting kills all roots
Rigid surviving bundles correspond to zero-dimensional/spherical -2 classes and serve as possible obstructions to the hope that twisting removes all roots.
Rigid surviving bundles give spherical/root classes that obstruct the hope that twisting removes all minus-two classes.
Rigid bundles are spherical/minimal-deformation root objects that can survive twisting, obstructing the idea that twisting removes all roots.
rigid vector bundles are spherical root objects with minimal deformation space that may survive twisting
Rigid bundles surviving the Brauer twist give spherical/root classes of square minus two, obstructing the naive hope that twisting removes all roots.
rigid bundles have zero-dimensional deformation theory/spherical minus-two classes and are invoked as roots that can survive twisting.
rigid bundles are zero-moduli spherical/root-type objects that can survive twisting and obstruct automatic root-freeness.

examples.name: Special matrix-factorization stability cases
examples.note: A question about Toda’s work leads to special potentials and matrix factorizations as known but non-general cases, illustrating the obstacle that stability conditions for arbitrary cubic K3 categories are not yet available.
